1.description: Dominated reverse convertible example. The figure displays the return to investors for two one-year reverse convertible bonds linked to the price of Microsoft Inc. provided that the principal has been converted into equity. JPMorgan Chase issued both bonds on June 30, 2008 (CUSIPS: 48123LAM6 and 48123LBR4). The reverse convertibles pay monthly coupons of 9.00% and 11.25%, respectively. If the share price of Microsoft Inc. ever closes below the convertible trigger price of $22.68 during the life of the bond, the bond principal is converted into equity and the issuer will pay the bondholder 35.27 shares of Microsoft Inc. per $1,000 invested ($1,000/Initial Price of Microsoft) rather than 100% of the principal amount invested. The final principal payment paid by the issuer is capped at par (100%).

2.description: Reverse convertible distribution. This figure displays the market structure/distribution process of the reverse convertible market.

3.description: Dispersion in implied value. This figure displays the dispersion in the implied value of reverse convertible bonds. I demean the implied value such that the average value is zero. The data set includes all U.S. SEC-registered one-year equity reverse convertible bonds issued over the period 2008 to 2012. I calculate the implied value of a reverse convertible as the cost of replicating its payoff according to equation1. In Panel A, the distribution is truncated at ± 10%. Panel A displays the dispersion in value for the full set of convertible bonds, whereas Panel B displays the dispersion across the set of convertible bonds that either unambiguously dominate or are dominated by at least one other convertible bond.

4.description: Issuance size versus implied value. This figure displays binned scatter plots of the implied value of a reverse convertible bond versus logged issuance size. I demean the implied value such that the average value is zero. The data set includes all U.S. SEC-registered one-year equity reverse convertible bonds issued over the period 2008 to 2012. I calculate the value of a reverse convertible as the cost of replicating its payoff according to equation1. Panel A is constructed using the full data set, whereas Panel B is constructed using the set of convertible bonds that either unambiguously dominate or are dominated by at least one other convertible bond.

5.description: Broker fees. Panel A displays the dispersion in broker fees. Panel B displays a binned scatter plot of broker fees versus the implied value of a reverse convertible bond. I demean implied value such that the average value is zero. The data set includes all U.S. SEC-registered one-year equity reverse convertible bonds issued over the period 2008 to 2012. I calculate the value of a reverse convertible as the cost of replicating its payoff according to equation1.

6.description: Issuance size versus implied value: Controlling for fees. This figure displays binned scatter plots of the implied value of a reverse convertible bond versus the logged issuance size, controlling for differences in broker fees. I demean implied value such that the average value is zero. The data set includes all U.S. SEC-registered one-year equity reverse convertible bonds issued over the period 2008 to 2012. I calculate the value of a reverse convertible as the cost of replicating its payoff according to equation1. Panel A is constructed using the full data set, whereas Panel B is constructed using the set of convertible bonds that either unambiguously dominate or are dominated by at least one other convertible bond.

7.description: Consumer search. This figure displays the estimated distribution of search costs and number of searches. Panel A displays the estimated distribution of search costs among sophisticated consumers. Panel B displays the estimated distribution of the number of times a consumer searches among sophisticated consumers. The estimates in Panel A and B correspond to the estimates reported in column (2) of TableIV. Full details on how the search cost distribution is recovered from the structural estimates are provided in the Appendix.

8.description: Issuer's markup. This figure displays the distribution of markups earned by issuers for creating reverse convertible bonds. I calculate the issuer's markup as the cost of constructing a short position in the reverse convertible (equation1) minus the broker fee,Markupi=EmbeddedOptionPremiumi−ValueofFixedRateBondi−Feei. The estimated markups assume no hedging costs or fixed issuance costs. Hedging and issuance costs could be roughly one to three percentage points, assuming a fixed issuance cost of $3,000 (author's estimate) and a hedging cost of 10% of the option value (Muravyev and Pearson (2015)). The data set includes all U.S. SEC-registered one-year equity reverse convertible bonds issued over the period 2008 to 2012.
